query:
FULL DICTATION for overread. Outside-hospital MRI abdomen with and without contrast, performed at a community center two days before transfer. I'm pasting the complete report exactly as it came to me so the handoff to our hepatobiliary team is clean. I need to know how critical results should be communicated per institutional policy when the imaging was performed at an outside facility and what documentation is required for an overread. Please read the whole dictation first before the question.

PATIENT BACKGROUND: 61-year-old woman, retired middle-school teacher, presented to an outside community hospital emergency department two days ago with two months of progressive fatigue, right upper quadrant discomfort worsening after meals, a 14 pound unintentional weight loss, and mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase. She was hemodynamically stable and not septic. She has no significant past medical history apart from well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 20 mg daily, and she denies tobacco use, reports moderate alcohol intake of one glass of wine most evenings, and has no known history of chronic viral hepatitis, autoimmune liver disease, or biliary disease. There is no personal history of cancer. Family history includes a mother who died at 72 of breast cancer with unknown BRCA status, a maternal aunt with pancreatic cancer in her 70s, and a father who is alive at 84 with stable coronary artery disease. She has three adult children, all well. She is sexually active only with her husband, has never had tattoos, has no remote travel or known parenteral exposures, received a full hepatitis A and B vaccine series as an adult, and has no injection drug use history.

LABS AT OUTSIDE HOSPITAL (from transfer packet, collected the day of presentation): AST 54 (normal up to 40), ALT 61 (normal up to 35), total bilirubin 1.4, direct bilirubin 0.6, alkaline phosphatase 216 (baseline four months earlier was 98), GGT 182, INR 1.1, platelets 212, albumin 3.8, AFP 11 (upper limit of normal 8.0), CA 19-9 54 (upper limit 35), CEA 3.2 (upper limit 5.0), hepatitis B surface antigen and surface antibody both negative, hepatitis C antibody negative, ANA 1:80 with no specific ANA subtyping, ceruloplasmin normal, ferritin 132, transferrin saturation 24%, alpha-1 antitrypsin normal. CBC was unremarkable. CMP notable only as above. TSH normal. HbA1c 5.5.

OUTSIDE HOSPITAL IMAGING TIMELINE: A right-upper-quadrant ultrasound was obtained first and reported a heterogeneous right hepatic lobe lesion concerning for mass versus complicated cyst, without free fluid or intrahepatic bile-duct dilation. MRI abdomen with and without gadoxetate disodium (Eovist) was then ordered on the same admission for characterization and was performed at the outside community center the following morning. The outside radiologist then released the final report, which I am reproducing below verbatim. After that, the patient was transferred to our system overnight so that hepatobiliary surgery and medical oncology could be engaged. The disk arrived with the patient this morning.

REPORT AS DICTATED AT OUTSIDE FACILITY, VERBATIM:

TECHNIQUE: 1.5T MRI abdomen performed on a 1.5T scanner (Siemens Avanto Fit per the DICOM header). Sequences included axial T1 in-phase and opposed-phase Dixon, axial T2 fat-suppressed, coronal single-shot T2 HASTE, axial diffusion-weighted imaging with b values of 50, 400, and 800 and corresponding ADC map, axial T1 fat-suppressed without contrast, and dynamic post-contrast T1 fat-suppressed imaging with gadoxetate disodium at 0.025 mmol/kg (7.5 mL for this 75 kg patient). Phases included precontrast, late arterial triggered using fluoro-triggering on the aorta, portal venous at 60 seconds, transitional at 3 minutes, and hepatobiliary phase at 20 minutes. MRCP was not acquired on this exam.

FINDINGS: There is a 9.2 x 7.6 x 6.9 cm dominant mass centered in segments VII and VIII of the right hepatic lobe. The mass is heterogeneous on T2, with central fluid-signal foci that do not restrict diffusion, peripheral isointense-to-hyperintense solid components, and no definite macroscopic fat on in-phase/out-of-phase imaging (no signal drop on opposed-phase). On DWI the solid rim demonstrates restricted diffusion with low ADC values quantitatively around 0.8 x 10^-3 mm^2/s. Dynamic post-contrast imaging shows early arterial peripheral nodular enhancement that does not fill in on portal venous phase, progressive internal enhancement through the transitional phase in a rim distribution, and lesional hypointensity relative to surrounding liver on the 20-minute hepatobiliary phase. Surrounding liver takes up gadoxetate normally, producing strong parenchymal hyperintensity at 20 minutes, which underscores the lesional hypointensity. A second, smaller 1.8 cm satellite lesion is seen in segment VI with similar imaging behavior. A third, more equivocal 7 mm focus in segment III is T2 slightly hyperintense, shows minimal restricted diffusion, and is hypointense on the hepatobiliary phase; it is indeterminate but suspicious in context.

VESSELS AND BILIARY: Main portal vein, right portal vein, and segmental branches patent. Hepatic veins and IVC patent. No definite tumor thrombus. Intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated.

OTHER: Pancreas: normal parenchyma, no focal mass, normal main pancreatic duct, no peripancreatic fluid. Adrenals: unremarkable. Kidneys: bilateral simple cortical cysts, largest 2.1 cm on the right with Bosniak II features. No solid renal mass. Gallbladder: distended without wall thickening or stones, no pericholecystic fluid. Bowel: normal caliber without obstruction. No free fluid. No retroperitoneal or portahepatic adenopathy. No peritoneal nodularity. Bones visualized without suspicious lesion.

IMPRESSION AS DICTATED AT OUTSIDE HOSPITAL: 1) Dominant heterogeneous 9.2 cm right hepatic lobe mass in segments VII and VIII, with a 1.8 cm satellite in segment VI and an indeterminate 7 mm focus in segment III. Imaging features (arterial peripheral nodular enhancement without fill-in, hepatobiliary phase hypointensity with normal background uptake, central necrosis with peripheral restricted diffusion) are worrisome for primary hepatic malignancy, with intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma favored given the hepatobiliary phase behavior; hepatocellular carcinoma is less likely but not excluded, and LI-RADS does not strictly apply in the absence of cirrhosis or known chronic viral hepatitis. Metastasis from an occult primary cannot be entirely excluded on imaging alone. Biopsy and multidisciplinary discussion recommended. 2) Bosniak II renal cysts; no solid renal mass. 3) Patent vasculature without tumor thrombus. 4) Non-dilated biliary tree.

END OF OUTSIDE REPORT.

COMMUNICATION HISTORY AT OUTSIDE HOSPITAL: The outside hospital radiologist paged the outside ED attending at the time of the read two days ago, spoke verbally, and documented the call with a timestamp in the outside report. The ED attending then contacted the admitting hospitalist at the outside facility. A decision was made to transfer to our center for hepatobiliary and oncology evaluation.

PATIENT STATUS NOW AT OUR HOSPITAL: She arrived overnight. She is currently on the general medicine floor under the accepting hospitalist. She is clinically stable. Labs overnight are similar to the outside transfer packet; her creatinine is 0.9 and eGFR is 78. She has had no imaging at our facility yet. The outside MRI was imported to our PACS as an 'outside studies' series with the report attached. I am the attending body imager assigned to overread the outside MRI this morning, with my overread released approximately 35 minutes ago into our institutional PACS and noted as concordant with the outside interpretation, with added language recommending triple-phase MRCP to further characterize the segment III lesion and to exclude a subtle ductal anatomic variation. Our hepatobiliary surgery and medical oncology teams have not yet been notified of our overread. The outpatient hepatobiliary clinic has a slot next week. The accepting hospitalist is aware that she is admitted for workup of a liver mass but has not read my overread yet.

RELEVANT INSTITUTIONAL WORKFLOW NOTES: Our system classifies newly identified suspected hepatic malignancy as a critical imaging finding when the lesion requires urgent multidisciplinary evaluation. Critical Results Communication Policy language governs timing of notification, required modalities, escalation paths, and documentation. The question below is specifically about the required action following an institutional overread that reproduces and refines an outside report's critical finding, given that the original communication chain occurred at the outside hospital.

INTERIM EVENTS SINCE TRANSFER: Upon arrival overnight, she underwent routine admission laboratory workup, which paralleled the outside transfer packet with the minor improvement that alkaline phosphatase had trended down slightly from 216 to 198, suggesting no acute worsening in biliary obstruction physiology. An IV was placed and she received maintenance fluids overnight. She slept about five hours, mildly uncomfortable from the new environment. Vital signs overnight were stable, with no fever, heart rate consistently in the 70s-80s, and blood pressures averaging 132/78. Pain was mild and managed with acetaminophen only; she declined opioid analgesia. Bowel movement was normal. Family (husband, one adult daughter) arrived this morning and are at the bedside, aware of the imaging findings and transfer rationale.

CURRENT CONSULTING SERVICES: Hepatobiliary surgery is aware at the service level (the transfer was accepted by their on-call fellow two days ago) but no specific attending has reviewed the imaging at our institution since arrival. Medical oncology is not yet consulted. Interventional radiology has not been consulted pending a final imaging assessment. Pathology has not been approached for biopsy workflow yet.

PRIOR ATTEMPTED COMMUNICATIONS BY PACS COORDINATOR: The PACS outside-studies coordinator was successful in importing the data. No direct call has been placed by our team to the outside radiologist because the transfer packet contains the full report. No direct call has been placed from our radiology team to the accepting hospitalist since the overread was signed 35 minutes ago. The institutional expectation is clear that imaging-team-initiated direct communication is required for critical findings; however, workflow ambiguity exists around overreads of already-communicated outside studies.

QUESTION: Under our institutional Critical Results Communication Policy, what am I required to do when I overread an outside imaging study and confirm a suspected malignancy that carries a critical-results designation? Specifically, please address: (a) whether the timeframe for critical-results communication resets when the study is re-interpreted by our institution and released into our PACS, (b) who counts as the appropriate responsible provider at our institution in this transfer-in scenario, (c) whether leaving a secure in-basket message in the electronic medical record or routing through a chart note is sufficient, or whether policy requires direct verbal communication, (d) what documentation must accompany the communication, and (e) what the escalation path is if the accepting hospitalist is unavailable by phone when called. Please cite the institutional policy directly rather than paraphrasing generically.
notes: OSR dictation - critical results for overread of outside imaging; ~1800 words.

query:
FULL DICTATION - complex abdominal CT with multiple incidental findings for the same patient. This is an outpatient staging CT with five separate incidental findings, each carrying different policy-compliant follow-up implications. The patient is under active oncology care so there are several competing workflow threads that make the disposition decision non-trivial. I need a finding-by-finding policy-compliant decision. Please read the entire dictation, not just the impression.

HISTORY: 67-year-old man with newly biopsy-proven stage IIA rectal adenocarcinoma (cT3 cN0 by rectal MRI already performed), at a mid-rectal location, signet-ring cell features absent, MSI-high per outside molecular testing. He is here for baseline staging CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis per the oncology service. No prior cross-sectional imaging at our institution. He has one prior outside chest X-ray and one outside screening colonoscopy five years ago which was normal before the current presentation.

PAST MEDICAL AND SURGICAL HISTORY: Type 2 diabetes on insulin glargine 18 units at bedtime and insulin lispro with meals, HbA1c 7.1% last month. Chronic kidney disease, stage 3a, with most recent eGFR 52, creatinine 1.4, stable for two years. Hypertension on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily. Hyperlipidemia on rosuvastatin 10 mg daily. Former tobacco use: quit 18 years ago, 22 pack-year history. Prior hernia repair and appendectomy remote. He has no prior contrast allergy and no known gadolinium reaction. He is not on metformin currently, having been held by his PCP due to CKD. He does not require premedication for today's iodinated contrast.

MEDICATIONS, ALLERGIES, SOCIAL: Medications as above plus once-daily aspirin 81 mg started two years ago for primary CV risk reduction. Allergies: NKDA. Social history: retired high-school industrial arts teacher, lives with spouse, two adult sons, moderate alcohol (one or two beers on weekends, no daily drinking), no recreational drug use. He has an active dog, no other pets. Family history: father with prostate cancer in his late 70s, mother with Alzheimer's disease, one brother with well-controlled Crohn's disease. No known familial colorectal cancer syndrome. Lynch syndrome panel pending at his oncologist's request given MSI-H status.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT abdomen and pelvis with IV and oral contrast, portal venous phase. IV contrast: iohexol 300 mg/mL, 130 mL, hand-administered via power injector at 3 mL/s. Oral contrast: dilute Gastrografin solution per patient-weight protocol, two servings one and two hours before. Low-dose chest CT added per institutional staging protocol, non-contrast chest is not our standard when abdomen is contrasted. Reconstructions: 3 mm axial at institutional standard kernel, 2 mm axial soft-tissue kernel, 1 mm lung kernel chest, coronal and sagittal 3 mm MPRs. Images reviewed on 3D workstation including volume-rendered overviews.

TECHNICAL QUALITY: Good opacification. No significant motion or beam-hardening artifact.

FINDINGS - PRIMARY RECTAL LESION: At approximately 8 cm from the anal verge, there is a circumferential rectal wall thickening extending over a 6 cm segment, consistent with the biopsy-proven adenocarcinoma. The lesion involves less than 50% of the circumference at its mid-portion with a broad base. Fat planes around the rectum are preserved; there is no extramural extension seen. The mesorectal fascia is not involved on the oblique coronal reformats (distance approximately 5 mm to the CRM at closest point). No enlarged mesorectal nodes by size criteria (threshold 8 mm short axis). Non-specific 5-6 mm nodes are present but below clinical size threshold for positive adenopathy. Correlation with the prior rectal MRI is advised for T-staging granularity.

LYMPH NODES: No significantly enlarged retroperitoneal, pelvic, or inguinal lymph nodes. A 7 mm pre-caval node is noted but below size threshold. No pathologic uptake or necrosis.

LIVER INCIDENTAL #1: There is a 2.6 cm arterially hyperenhancing lesion in segment VII not fully characterized on this portal-venous-only staging protocol. A few scattered sub-centimeter hypodense lesions in both lobes are too small to characterize. In a patient with newly staged GI malignancy, a 2.6 cm lesion that cannot be dismissed as benign without MRI characterization warrants dedicated imaging before staging can be finalized. An MRI liver with hepatobiliary contrast (Eovist) is the recommended next study for this lesion.

ADRENAL INCIDENTAL #2: The right adrenal gland contains a 2.2 cm well-circumscribed lesion with homogeneous attenuation measured at -6 HU on portal venous phase. Single-phase density in the macroscopic fat range is highly suggestive of a benign adenoma and does not require dedicated washout CT or MRI in this context. Consider noting in the final impression as consistent with adenoma, no further imaging required.

RENAL INCIDENTAL #3: Bilateral simple cortical cysts, all Bosniak I. A 3.1 cm left renal lesion has thin septations and mild enhancement of the septa. This characterizes as Bosniak IIF and warrants follow-up MR or CT in 6-12 months. Creatinine at time of scan 1.4, eGFR 52. No solid renal mass.

PANCREAS, SPLEEN, BOWEL: Pancreas normal. Spleen 10.4 cm, homogeneous. Bowel without obstruction or inflammation elsewhere. Outside ultrasound one month ago reportedly mentioned a 2.1 cm echogenic splenic lesion which is not seen on today's CT; if correlation is desired, dedicated MRI or follow-up US would be reasonable.

VASCULATURE: Mild atherosclerotic calcifications in the aorta and iliac arteries. Infrarenal aorta measures 2.4 cm transverse. No aneurysm, no significant stenosis on the reviewed segments.

BONES INCIDENTAL #4: A 1.3 cm sclerotic focus in the left iliac bone, without matrix mineralization, is present. Size and appearance are most consistent with a bone island; however, metastasis cannot be completely excluded without further characterization. In a patient with a newly diagnosed rectal cancer but staging only T3 cN0, an isolated sclerotic lesion is not an expected metastatic site. No other suspicious osseous lesion in the visualized skeleton. Consider noting in the impression and correlating with oncology staging decisions.

LUNG BASES AND CHEST (from chest CT add-on) INCIDENTAL #5: A 9 mm solid non-calcified nodule is present in the right lower lobe. No prior imaging exists at our institution for comparison; an outside chest X-ray three years ago was reportedly normal. The nodule does not have benign features (no visible calcification, no fat). Fleischner Society follow-up is indicated for a 9 mm incidental solid nodule in a former smoker: typically follow-up CT chest at 3 months, 9-12 months, and 18-24 months, unless prior imaging establishes stability. No other lung nodules, no mediastinal adenopathy.

OTHER: Degenerative changes in the lumbar spine. No acute fracture. Incidental IVC filter from a prior event in another chart (not flagged in chart summary) is not present on this scan (confirming no retained device). Gallbladder contains a single dependent calcification consistent with a stone, no cholecystitis.

IMPRESSION: 1) Known mid-rectal adenocarcinoma, cT3 appearance, no evident metastatic adenopathy or distant metastasis at the moment. 2) Five incidental findings as above, each carrying different follow-up expectations. 3) Recommend MRI liver with Eovist for the segment VII arterially enhancing lesion before finalizing staging. 4) Chest finding should follow Fleischner Society guidance with first CT in 3 months unless prior imaging establishes stability.

COMMUNICATION: The oncology team has not yet been contacted. The patient is in the outpatient imaging suite and the oncology clinic visit is in two days. No critical-flag communication has been initiated yet from our side.

PROVIDER RELATIONSHIPS AND ROUTINE COMMUNICATION: The ordering medical oncologist is well known to our radiology department and is part of the hospital's GI oncology multidisciplinary conference, which meets weekly. Routine imaging reports reach the oncologist through the signed radiology report posted to the electronic chart and via the automated results-review task. Abnormal-but-not-critical findings are typically handled by the standard electronic routing. Institutional policy distinguishes between routine results routed electronically and critical findings that require direct communication; however, the boundary between 'incidental finding of unclear clinical significance' and 'critical finding requiring immediate verbal communication' can be ambiguous in oncology contexts where the incidental finding might plausibly represent metastatic disease. Internal departmental practice has favored erring toward direct verbal communication for any imaging finding that could plausibly be metastatic in a patient with active oncologic disease.

OPERATIONAL STATUS OF OUTPATIENT IMAGING: The outpatient imaging center at our campus processes approximately 200-250 oncology staging studies per week. The average turnaround for imaging-team-initiated communication to oncology is under 30 minutes for critical findings and the department tracks compliance as a quality metric. There is a standing hot-line number for the on-call oncologist during business hours to ensure reliable critical-finding handoffs. After hours the process routes through the hospital operator to the oncology on-call fellow or attending.

ADDITIONAL CLINICAL-DETAIL REMINDERS: The patient has not yet been told about the multiple incidentals. He was told at intake that the scan would be a baseline staging study. The chart contains a patient-preference note indicating the patient prefers to hear significant imaging findings from his primary oncologist rather than from radiology directly.

QUESTION: Five incidentals need disposition under our institutional Critical Results Communication Policy. For each of (1) 2.6 cm segment VII arterially hyperenhancing liver lesion in a new GI malignancy, (2) 2.2 cm right adrenal adenoma appearance, (3) 3.1 cm Bosniak IIF renal lesion, (4) 1.3 cm sclerotic iliac focus, and (5) 9 mm solid lung nodule with no prior imaging: should the finding trigger formal critical-results notification with direct verbal communication and documentation, be documented in the impression only, or be handled through routine outpatient follow-up? Please walk through each individually, cite the institutional policy for anything requiring direct communication, and be explicit where the institutional policy does not address an incidental category and the recommendation is based on standard radiology practice rather than local policy.
notes: Multi-incidental abdominal CT; per-finding policy-compliant disposition; ~1800 words.

query:
FULL DICTATION - stroke MRI with a time-critical institutional communication decision. I need help interpreting our institutional critical-results communication requirement for an acute stroke MRI read from the emergency department in a thrombectomy-candidate scenario, including what counts as the responsible provider, what counts as satisfactory communication, and the required escalation path. Full dictation and operational context below.

PATIENT: 72-year-old right-handed retired schoolteacher, independent at baseline, lives with spouse of 45 years, no known cognitive impairment or frailty concerns. She presented to our ED at 07:14 via EMS with sudden-onset right facial droop and expressive aphasia noted by family at 06:40, last known well at 06:20 when her husband left for a morning walk. NIHSS at triage was 14 (aphasia, right facial weakness, right arm drift, right leg drift, partial neglect, mild dysarthria). She denies headache and chest pain. She is not on any immunosuppression. She has a witnessed episode of difficulty expressing words over breakfast that progressed over approximately 20 minutes to frank expressive aphasia, followed by right-sided weakness.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: Atrial fibrillation on apixaban 5 mg BID (she missed her last two doses per her husband; the spouse reports her pillbox for the last two days still has the apixaban tablets in place). Hypertension on losartan 50 mg daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily. Hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 40 mg daily. No prior stroke, no prior TIA documented in our chart (she does have one visit from 5 years ago with symptoms that resolved before arrival and were not further worked up, per her PCP's note; a 'spell' not further classified). No diabetes. No active cancer. No recent surgery, no recent trauma. No known coagulopathy. No baseline renal impairment (eGFR at most recent clinic visit 68). She has never smoked. She has no alcohol use beyond a small wine at holidays.

MEDICATIONS, ALLERGIES, DEVICES: Apixaban 5 mg BID (missed x2). Losartan 50 mg daily. Amlodipine 5 mg daily. Atorvastatin 40 mg daily. Multivitamin. No NSAIDs chronically. No steroids. No narcotics. Allergies: shellfish (self-reported hives decades ago, not verified), NKDA otherwise, no known contrast allergy. Devices: none. No pacemaker, no implanted hardware, no retained metallic fragments. Dental bridges only.

ED WORKUP TIMELINE: Triage 07:14. Non-contrast head CT at 07:32, read by neuroradiology and reported as without hemorrhage, without loss of gray-white, with a subtle hyperdense left MCA vessel sign. CT angiogram head and neck at 07:35, read as a left M1 occlusion with no proximal cervical source. CT perfusion at 07:40 suggested a left MCA territory ischemic penumbra with mismatch, specifically an ischemic core by CBF < 30% estimated at approximately 18 mL and a Tmax > 6 s territory estimated at approximately 62 mL, yielding a mismatch ratio substantially greater than 1.8. IV thrombolytic therapy was considered; however, the missed apixaban doses, with the last confirmed dose approximately 50 hours before arrival, generated uncertainty about residual anticoagulant effect. The ED attending in consultation with stroke neurology chose to defer IV tPA and proceed with MRI and endovascular evaluation rather than rely on perfusion alone.

MRI TECHNIQUE: 3T MRI brain. Sequences: axial and coronal DWI at b=1000 and ADC map, axial FLAIR, axial GRE, axial T1, axial T2, and 3D time-of-flight MRA circle of Willis. No contrast administered. Patient cooperated adequately despite aphasia with her husband at bedside prior to scan. Scan time approximately 10 minutes, completed at 08:19. Images available on PACS at 08:21.

FINDINGS: DWI and ADC map demonstrate a large left MCA territory acute infarct. The ADC abnormality involves approximately 60% of the left MCA territory including insular ribbon, opercular cortex, lateral putamen, body of caudate, and anterior temporal cortex. The DWI bright territory is approximately 75 mL by semi-automated volumetry. FLAIR shows early parenchymal signal changes within a subregion of the DWI abnormality, but a substantial portion of the DWI bright territory remains DWI-FLAIR mismatched, supporting a still-recent infarct in a majority of the ADC abnormal tissue. GRE does not demonstrate blooming microhemorrhages in the infarcted territory, and no acute macroscopic hemorrhagic transformation is present. MRA shows an abrupt cutoff of the left M1 segment approximately 1.2 cm from the left ICA bifurcation with minimal distal reconstitution and essentially no visualized M2 branches. Contralateral right MCA circulation and anterior cerebral arteries appear intact. Posterior circulation is intact including posterior communicating arteries. No hemorrhage anywhere. No mass effect beyond expected from the infarct. No midline shift. No acute hydrocephalus. Chronic small-vessel ischemic changes are present in the periventricular white matter bilaterally, consistent with age and chronic hypertension.

IMPRESSION: 1) Acute left MCA territory infarct with a large area of DWI-FLAIR mismatch consistent with a still-recent infarct core and substantial surrounding penumbra. 2) Left M1 occlusion consistent with large-vessel occlusion suitable for mechanical thrombectomy consideration. 3) No hemorrhage. 4) No secondary mass effect requiring neurosurgical decompression at this time.

COMMUNICATION: The MRI was read by the attending neuroradiologist (me) at 08:21. My dictation was signed at 08:26. The patient is being transferred back to the emergency department. The ED attending who ordered the study is actively caring for a separate patient with chest pain plus two lower-acuity patients. The interventional neuroradiology team is currently in the angio suite with a different case expected to end in approximately 30-40 minutes. The stroke neurology fellow is on the floor and has been paged but not yet responded. The patient's husband is in the ED waiting room. No other family members are present.

RECENT POLICY QUESTIONS AT OUR INSTITUTION: Our system has recently emphasized direct verbal communication of critical findings and the expectation that electronic messages and chart notes, while useful for documentation, do not by themselves satisfy the policy. The policy has specific language governing escalation if the responsible provider cannot be reached, and specifies a maximum time from interpretation to verbal contact. The question below is about application of the policy to this thrombectomy-candidate read where the ordering ED attending is occupied, the stroke neurology team has been paged, and the interventional team is in another case.

ADDITIONAL NIHSS DETAIL: The full NIHSS 14 breaks down as follows: level of consciousness 0, LOC questions 1, LOC commands 0, gaze 0, visual fields 1, facial palsy 2, left arm 0, right arm 3, left leg 0, right leg 2, limb ataxia untestable, sensory 1, best language 3, dysarthria 0, extinction/inattention 1. Her baseline modified Rankin score was 0. She lives independently, drives, manages her own medications, and volunteers at the local library three afternoons per week.

ADDITIONAL ED TIMELINE AND VITAL-SIGN TRAJECTORY: Triage vital signs 07:14 BP 168/94, HR 94, RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air. CT stroke protocol 07:32. tPA decision deferred at 07:44 in consultation with stroke neurology. MRI performed 08:04-08:19. On return from MRI at 08:30 her blood pressure is 142/88 after dihydralazine 10 mg IV. Neurological exam at 08:30 unchanged from triage.

ANTICOAGULATION AND ANTIDOTE CONSIDERATIONS: Apixaban last-dose timing is critical for thrombolytic decision making; it was approximately 50 hours before presentation. At standard half-life apixaban should be largely cleared in this timeframe. Andexanet alfa is available in our pharmacy for emergency reversal should hemorrhagic transformation develop after thrombectomy. Four-factor prothrombin complex concentrate is also available as a bridge.

INTERVENTIONAL AND ANESTHESIA LOGISTICS: The angiography suite is currently in use with an intracranial aneurysm coiling case expected to finish in 30-40 minutes. The neurointerventional attending is in that case. The second neurointerventional attending on backup call is 20 minutes away. Anesthesia is staffed in the angio suite.

FAMILY CONTEXT: Her husband arrived with her and is in the ED waiting room, aware of the MRI and anxious for information. Two adult children have been called and are en route from neighboring cities. The patient's advance directive is on file; it designates her husband as healthcare proxy and specifies a preference for full aggressive care if meaningful recovery is expected. The stroke team has not yet discussed thrombectomy risk-benefit with the family.

FAMILY HISTORY AND LONG-TERM MEDICATIONS REVIEW: She has a sister with coronary artery disease s/p stenting and a brother with well-controlled type 2 diabetes. No family history of stroke at early age. No family history of hereditary thrombophilia. Her apixaban was started three years ago after a first documented episode of atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response; prior to that she was on low-dose aspirin. Her statin was started approximately 10 years ago after an abnormal lipid panel. Her losartan replaced an ACE-inhibitor four years ago after an ACE-related cough.

PRE-HOSPITAL AND EMS HANDOFF: EMS documentation indicates the call was placed at 06:47 by the husband. EMS arrived at 06:58. Vital signs on scene: BP 178/96, HR 88, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, glucose 128. IV access was established in the left antecubital. The last-known-well was confirmed at 06:20 by the husband per direct questioning. Arrival at our ED at 07:14.

QUESTION: Under our institutional Critical Results Communication Policy, what is the required action for this read? Please specify: (1) Is an acute LVO with DWI-FLAIR mismatch and thrombectomy candidacy a critical result under our policy? (2) What is the maximum allowed time between image interpretation and verbal communication to the responsible provider? (3) If the ED attending who ordered the study is occupied and cannot come to the phone, what does the policy require - voicemail, paging, escalation path, or something else? (4) Does leaving a chart note, secure message, or electronic stroke alert satisfy the communication requirement? (5) What documentation is required to close the loop? Please cite the policy explicitly.

query:
ED consult pasted from secure chat. Patient is downstairs waiting for a protocol decision and the ED attending wants the right study ordered once. Please read the full consult and tell me the best imaging protocol given the competing constraints. The right answer is not obvious because there are multiple contraindications in play.

PATIENT BACKGROUND: 67-year-old man, 82 kg, presenting with four days of worsening left flank and left lower quadrant pain, subjective fevers to 102°F at home, and dysuria. Urinalysis shows pyuria with many WBCs and moderate bacteria. Urine culture pending. CBC shows WBC 17.4 with left shift, lactate 2.1 on arrival and now 2.6 on repeat two hours later. CMP notable for creatinine 2.9 (baseline 1.8 per patient report), eGFR 22. Procalcitonin 2.4. BP 104/68 on arrival, heart rate 104, afebrile in the ED so far but rigoring intermittently. After 1 L normal saline his BP is 112/70 and lactate is 2.6.

DEVICES AND CARDIOVASCULAR HISTORY: Mechanical aortic valve replacement 11 years ago (St Jude Regent bileaflet, documentation of MR-conditionality confirmed in our device registry; the original op note is scanned in the chart). He is on warfarin with most recent INR 2.6 in the therapeutic range. He has coronary artery disease with PCI and a bare-metal stent in the mid-LAD 8 years ago; this is MR-conditional at 3T after 6 weeks (well beyond the hardware window). No pacemaker. No ICD. No left ventricular assist device. No intracranial stents. No other indwelling hardware.

RENAL AND CONTRAST HISTORY: CKD stage 4 known baseline, creatinine baseline 1.8, eGFR baseline 32, with acute-on-chronic rise today to creatinine 2.9 and eGFR 22 most likely from sepsis and pre-renal. Never on dialysis. No transplant. He had a prior severe iodinated contrast reaction six years ago requiring ED admission: diffuse urticaria, wheezing, transient hypotension with BP nadir 82/55, treated with IV diphenhydramine, IV methylprednisolone, nebulized albuterol, and a single IM epinephrine 0.3 mg dose; he was not intubated. Since then he has avoided iodinated contrast. He has never had gadolinium documented in our system; his prior MRI of the knee outside was non-contrast. He does not have a known gadolinium allergy.

OTHER PAST MEDICAL HISTORY: Hypertension on metoprolol succinate 50 mg BID (the beta-blocker complicates the risk profile of re-exposure to iodinated contrast); hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 40 mg daily; type 2 diabetes on insulin glargine and lispro, HbA1c 7.3%; BPH on tamsulosin; remote GERD on PPI; no known cognitive impairment. Former smoker, quit 14 years ago, 20 pack-year history. Alcohol: one drink on social occasions. No recreational drug use.

ALLERGIES: Iodinated contrast (severe, as above). Penicillin (mild rash 30 years ago, not verified). Shellfish unclear. No latex. NKDA otherwise.

CURRENT SUSPICION: Suspected left perinephric abscess versus severe pyelonephritis with possible early abscess, possibly with urinary obstruction. The ED team wants a cross-sectional study to confirm or exclude drainable collection and to identify obstruction that might change immediate management including ureteral stenting versus percutaneous nephrostomy. Urology is en route for possible drainage. Nephrology has been paged and is en route.

COMPETING MODALITY ANALYSIS:
1. Iodinated contrast CT (with accelerated premedication): The only regimen compatible with the clinical urgency is the accelerated 5-hour regimen using IV methylprednisolone 40 mg or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg and IV diphenhydramine 50 mg, but even with premedication a prior severe reaction that required IM epinephrine is not a no-risk re-exposure. Beta-blocker use complicates management of a breakthrough reaction because epinephrine response may be attenuated and glucagon may be needed. Additionally eGFR 22 raises contrast-induced nephropathy risk in acute-on-chronic kidney injury. Aggressive hydration, holding the ACE-inhibitor-type agents (not applicable here since he is on metoprolol, not an ACE-I), and close observation would be needed.
2. Gadolinium-enhanced MRI (Group II macrocyclic, smallest diagnostic dose): eGFR 22 places him in the high-risk band for nephrogenic systemic fibrosis (NSF). Current ACR guidance recognizes Group II agents have extremely low NSF risk even in eGFR < 30, and for a clinically necessary study the benefit-risk can favor imaging with documentation. Logistics: MRI turnaround at our facility is approximately 45 minutes plus scan time. For suspected abscess, MRI with DWI and post-contrast imaging has good sensitivity.
3. Non-contrast MRI with DWI and T2: Good sensitivity for collection detection and hydronephrosis without any contrast. Logistics comparable to option 2 scanner time. Limited tissue characterization compared with post-contrast imaging.
4. Ultrasound: feasible for hydronephrosis, less sensitive for abscess, limited by body habitus and left flank pain in a rigoring patient.
5. Non-contrast CT: immediately available, identifies obstruction and hydronephrosis and many abscesses, but less sensitive for small early collections and does not characterize vascular integrity or early phlegmon distinctly from abscess.

OPERATIONAL AND TIME CONSTRAINTS: CT is immediately available. MRI has a 45-minute wait. He is stable but septic. Lactate 2.6. The surgical/urological team wants to act within the next 60-90 minutes if drainable collection is present. Anesthesia is available if sedation is needed for MRI.

ADDITIONAL CONSIDERATIONS: His valve is fully MR-conditional at 3T. His BMS is also well past the 6-week window and is MR-safe. He does not have intracranial clips. He has not had MRI since his last device implant; however, MR safety documentation is intact. He has mild claustrophobia but has previously tolerated outside MRI with oral lorazepam 0.5 mg pre-scan. His INR on warfarin is 2.6 which does not contraindicate MRI or CT but might change any periprocedural bleeding planning for drainage. Anticoagulation management would be handled by the admitting team in discussion with urology.

DETAILED RENAL TRAJECTORY: Creatinine trend: baseline 1.8 three months ago at PCP visit, 1.9 one month ago, 2.1 two weeks ago during a routine follow-up, 2.4 yesterday at an outside urgent-care visit before ED arrival, 2.9 today. The acute-on-chronic pattern is consistent with pre-renal AKI secondary to sepsis and mild dehydration. Urine output over the last 6 hours in the ED is approximately 40 mL/hour, which is adequate but not robust. Urine studies suggest pre-renal physiology with FENa <1% and a bland sediment. Nephrology's preliminary phone input favored aggressive hydration, holding nephrotoxic medications, and proceeding with contrast only if the clinical question cannot be answered otherwise. Nephrology asked that the radiology team document the benefit-risk decision.

PRIOR CONTRAST REACTION DOCUMENTATION: The severe reaction six years ago has detailed documentation in the allergy tab. The ED note from that admission recorded diffuse urticaria, wheezing, and a nadir blood pressure of 82/55 that was treated with IV diphenhydramine 50 mg, IV methylprednisolone 125 mg, nebulized albuterol, and a single 0.3 mg IM epinephrine injection. Subsequent observation was 8 hours. Discharge was with a detailed allergy-update note instructing future avoidance of iodinated contrast if possible and, if unavoidable, premedication plus anesthesia standby.

BETA-BLOCKER MANAGEMENT: His metoprolol succinate 50 mg BID has been held since admission per the admitting team's protocol for patients with active sepsis physiology. Beta-blocker withdrawal 24-48 hours before contrast administration has been proposed in the literature to improve responsiveness to epinephrine should a breakthrough anaphylactoid reaction occur. Glucagon 1 mg IV is available as adjunctive therapy for beta-blocker-unresponsive anaphylaxis; pharmacy has been alerted.

DEVICE INTERROGATION: His mechanical valve requires no interrogation. His BMS requires no interrogation. If MRI proceeds at 3T, both are MR-conditional per device documentation in our chart.

FURTHER CROSS-DEPARTMENTAL COORDINATION: The admitting team is the internal-medicine hospitalist service; the admitting attending is currently on rounds elsewhere in the hospital. Urology is the primary procedural service for a drainable collection; they are 15 minutes away and have cleared their afternoon for a possible percutaneous drainage or cystoscopic stent placement. Interventional radiology is available as backup for image-guided drainage if it is preferred by the urology team. Anesthesia is available bedside if deep sedation is needed for any MRI or drainage procedure. Nephrology has been contacted by the hospitalist and is coming within the next hour.

PRIOR IMAGING AT OUR INSTITUTION: CT abdomen and pelvis with iodinated contrast 8 years ago at our institution (before his contrast reaction) was unremarkable. MRI knee at 1.5T outside two years ago tolerated without issue. No prior MRI of the abdomen. Ultrasound abdomen 3 years ago showed normal kidneys with no hydronephrosis and a mildly echogenic liver consistent with fatty infiltration. No prior gadolinium at our institution. Renal ultrasound 1 year ago showed CKD-pattern echogenicity and normal cortical thickness.

CLINICAL QUESTION FRAMING: The urologist specifically wants to know: (1) is there a drainable collection, (2) is there proximal urinary obstruction requiring urgent decompression, and (3) is there evidence of emphysematous infection. Each of these questions can be partially answered by non-contrast imaging but the question of abscess versus phlegmon and the question of small early collections requires contrast-enhanced imaging.

QUESTION: Given the competing renal, contrast-allergy, beta-blocker-modified anaphylaxis risk, MR-compatible device history, and clinical-urgency constraints, (a) what is the optimal single imaging protocol to obtain in the next hour, (b) walk through the reasoning comparing iodinated-with-accelerated-premedication (with beta-blocker caveat) versus gadolinium-with-macrocyclic (with eGFR 22 caveat) versus non-contrast modalities, (c) state the preferred approach along with the acceptable alternative if the preferred path is unavailable, and (d) name the documentation required for the institutional record of contrast decisioning in a patient with a prior severe reaction and acute-on-chronic CKD. Please cite the institutional renal-function-for-contrast-administration policy in the answer.

sourceMustInclude: Renal Function Assessment for Contrast Administration

notes: ED consult protocol with renal + severe allergy + beta-blocker + valve/stent competing constraints; ~1800 words.

query:
ED consult, urgent protocol question. Pregnant patient with suspected pulmonary embolism and a prior moderate contrast reaction history. Whole consult pasted below. Please give the preferred imaging and the premedication decision for the chosen study, with explicit attention to radiation, breast dose, and institutional versus national guidance.

PATIENT BACKGROUND: 32-year-old G2P1 at 26 weeks 3 days by LMP, dating confirmed on first-trimester US. Presented to our ED with three hours of progressive pleuritic right-sided chest pain and mild dyspnea, unable to lie flat in bed. No prior DVT, no OCP exposure (she has been off OCPs for years given planned pregnancy then actual pregnancy). No recent travel beyond short trips to visit family in a neighboring state, no prolonged immobilization, no trauma. She had her first child uneventfully four years ago by vaginal delivery.

VITALS: BP 118/72 on arrival, HR 108 regular, RR 22, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 37.1°C. After 20 minutes in triage her SpO2 drifted to 94% on room air, prompting upgrade to a more acute ED bay and placement of 2 L nasal cannula restoring her SpO2 to 98%. She is uncomfortable but conversant. No calf swelling or asymmetry by bedside physical exam, but the ED resident specifically notes that physical-exam sensitivity for DVT is low.

LABS: D-dimer is elevated as expected in pregnancy; a modified obstetric D-dimer threshold is not available at our hospital. CBC notable for mild leukocytosis WBC 12.8. CMP with creatinine 0.6, eGFR > 90, potassium 3.9, normal liver enzymes. Troponin pending. BNP pending. Lactate 1.4 normal. Fibrinogen elevated as expected. INR 1.0. Type and screen pending. Lower-extremity compression ultrasound has been ordered and is scheduled for approximately 90 minutes from now; the tech is finishing a different study. ECG shows sinus tachycardia without S1Q3T3 and without ischemic changes.

OBSTETRIC HISTORY: One prior uneventful pregnancy and vaginal delivery four years ago. Current pregnancy has been uncomplicated so far. Normal anatomy ultrasound at 20 weeks. No placenta previa. No gestational diabetes. No preeclampsia features at recent prenatal visits. She is followed at our OB clinic and records are current in our chart. No history of bleeding disorder. No family history of hereditary thrombophilia that she is aware of. She has no heparin-induced thrombocytopenia history.

CONTRAST HISTORY: One prior moderate iodinated contrast reaction five years ago during a CT pulmonary angiogram for rule-out PE (study was negative). She had diffuse urticaria, mild wheezing, and was observed in the ED with IV diphenhydramine and hydrocortisone; no intubation, no epinephrine required per the documented ED note. She has had no other iodinated contrast since then. No known gadolinium reaction and no prior gadolinium exposure documented in our system. Mild intermittent asthma, not using an inhaler regularly, no ICU admissions, no steroid burst in the last year. No latex allergy. Baseline eGFR normal at most recent outpatient labs.

MEDICATIONS AND ALLERGIES: Prenatal vitamins. Labetalol 200 mg BID for mild gestational hypertension started three weeks ago. Acetaminophen as needed. Allergies: penicillin (rash only), iodinated contrast moderate reaction as above, shellfish unclear. No other medications. No NSAIDs in pregnancy.

PROTOCOL OPTIONS UNDER CONSIDERATION:
1. CTPA with accelerated iodinated premedication regimen. Radiation dose to the fetus is estimated less than 0.1 mGy at 26 weeks with modern low-dose protocol, well within accepted pregnancy imaging exposure thresholds. Maternal breast dose is a concern at 26 weeks, estimated 20-40 mGy with standard protocols; breast shielding can reduce effective breast dose by 30-50% without significant image degradation in pulmonary arteries. Premedication using the 5-hour accelerated regimen must be completed quickly given the acute clinical suspicion - IV methylprednisolone 40 mg or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg now and IV diphenhydramine 50 mg 1 hour before contrast. An emergency single-dose regimen with IV hydrocortisone 200 mg plus IV diphenhydramine 50 mg immediately before contrast could be used if the 5-hour window cannot be met.
2. V/Q scan, specifically perfusion-only to reduce fetal dose. Advantages: no iodinated contrast, lower fetal radiation dose than CTPA on most protocols depending on methodology. Disadvantages: nuclear medicine turnaround in our ED setting is typically 2-3 hours, availability depends on the on-call nuclear tech. Chest X-ray should ideally precede V/Q for interpretation context. Ventilation component may not be required in a patient with a normal chest X-ray per some institutional guidance. Fetal dose estimates for perfusion-only low-dose protocols are approximately 0.1-0.37 mGy. Breast dose is substantially lower than CTPA.
3. MR pulmonary angiogram without contrast. Evolving technique, not first-line for suspected acute PE at our institution, reserved for selected cases with both contrast contraindications and V/Q unavailability. Longer protocol with breath-holds that may be challenging for a tachypneic patient.
4. Lower-extremity compression ultrasound first; if positive for DVT, PE can be inferred for treatment purposes and CTPA can be avoided entirely. Current turnaround approximately 90 minutes. If negative, does not exclude PE.
5. Empiric anticoagulation pending imaging confirmation. Could be started with weight-based subcutaneous low-molecular-weight heparin (enoxaparin) if clinical suspicion is high enough to not wait for imaging, in consultation with OB.

CLINICAL SUSPICION LEVEL: Modified Wells criteria in pregnancy are controversial. Her symptoms plus tachycardia plus desaturation to 94% on room air and requirement for supplemental oxygen push this above a low-probability presentation. The ED team considers her intermediate-to-high pretest probability.

OBSTETRIC AND OPERATIONAL DETAILS: The patient is stable but symptomatic. The OB team has been contacted and agrees with urgent workup and is comfortable with maternal anticoagulation if warranted. She is able to take oral medications. She would like the study done today and is anxious about contrast because of the prior reaction. Her husband is in the waiting room. She consents to imaging but wants to minimize fetal exposure where clinically reasonable.

RELEVANT INSTITUTIONAL CONSIDERATIONS: Our institutional Contrast Reaction Management Protocol covers premedication regimens but is silent on pregnancy-specific modality choice for suspected PE; the radiology on-call must integrate that with the pregnancy-imaging sections of the practice and with current society guidelines.

DEEPER OBSTETRIC CONTEXT: Her prior pregnancy four years ago was uncomplicated aside from mild gestational anemia managed with oral iron. Labor and delivery were unremarkable at 39 weeks and 3 days. She breastfed successfully for 11 months. Her mother had one pregnancy complication with preeclampsia at term; her older sister had a postpartum DVT approximately 4 weeks after delivery at age 34. Hereditary thrombophilia testing has not been performed; it is typically deferred until after a provoked or unprovoked VTE given the imprecise implications.

FETAL DOSE ESTIMATES BY PROTOCOL: Her OB and radiology teams have reviewed the trimester-specific fetal-dose estimates for the candidate imaging modalities. For a standard CTPA at 26 weeks using our current low-dose pregnancy protocol, the estimated fetal dose is 0.06-0.10 mGy. Maternal breast dose is approximately 20-30 mGy without shielding and 12-18 mGy with breast shielding. For perfusion-only V/Q using a reduced-dose technetium-labeled MAA protocol, the estimated fetal dose is 0.10-0.37 mGy depending on the injected activity and bladder clearance kinetics. Neither modality carries meaningful teratogenic risk at 26 weeks given the organogenesis window has closed.

RADIOLOGY ON-CALL CONTEXT: The on-call radiologist is present in the reading room; the nuclear medicine tech is on call but not yet in house (arrival in approximately 45 minutes if paged). The CT scanner is available immediately and the accelerated premedication regimen could be initiated now with IV methylprednisolone 40 mg (or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg) plus IV diphenhydramine 50 mg one hour before contrast; imaging could be performed 1-2 hours from now with appropriate supervision.

ANTICOAGULATION CONSIDERATIONS IF PE IS CONFIRMED: Weight-based enoxaparin (1 mg/kg subcutaneous twice daily) is the treatment of choice in pregnancy. Warfarin is teratogenic and contraindicated. Direct oral anticoagulants are not recommended in pregnancy. If the LE ultrasound is positive and PE is inferred, treatment can be initiated immediately without CTPA.

ADDITIONAL INTERDISCIPLINARY CONTEXT: Her OB attending physician is present in the hospital today covering labor and delivery and is a 5-minute response to any call. The high-risk OB consultant has been messaged for routine input. The maternal-fetal medicine fellow is familiar with pregnancy-specific imaging protocols and is available to advise on fetal dose estimates. The anesthesia team has been alerted in case contrast-premedication plus potential anaphylaxis management at the scanner requires airway readiness.

PATIENT PREFERENCE AND COMMUNICATION: The patient is anxious but is a medically literate person (she is a nurse at a neighboring hospital) and has specifically asked whether any imaging can be done without iodinated contrast. She has asked about fetal dose ranges and wants the numbers in context. She is comfortable with the idea of starting anticoagulation if imaging cannot be obtained safely.

QUESTION: Given the pregnancy at 26 weeks, the prior moderate iodinated contrast reaction, the acute and intermediate-to-high PE suspicion, and the pending lower-extremity US, (a) what is the preferred imaging strategy in the next hour and why, (b) if CTPA ultimately is needed, what is the correct accelerated premedication regimen including doses and timing, (c) what is the safe alternative if premedication cannot be completed in time, (d) how should fetal and maternal-breast dose be communicated to the patient, and (e) what documentation should the radiology team leave in the record about the modality decision? Be explicit that the radiologist is not the treating physician and that institutional protocol takes precedence over general textbook statements.
notes: Pregnancy + PE + contrast allergy triple-constraint protocol selection; ~1800 words.

query:
ED consult. Urgent imaging decision for suspected spinal epidural abscess in a patient with multiple imaging contraindications. Please read the full consult and make a protocol recommendation, taking into account claustrophobia, eGFR, allergy, and device history simultaneously.

PATIENT BACKGROUND: 54-year-old woman, 89 kg, BMI 32, presenting with six days of progressive midline lower back pain, new bilateral lower-extremity weakness (4/5 hip flexion, 4-/5 dorsiflexion bilateral), saddle paresthesia, and new urinary retention as of today. She is afebrile but WBC is 14.8 with 86% neutrophils. ESR 82. CRP 14.5. Blood cultures drawn and pending. Neurosurgery is aware and has seen the patient. Epidural abscess is the leading concern and surgical decompression may be needed urgently if confirmed.

NEUROLOGIC EXAM: She is alert, fully oriented, with normal language and cranial nerves. Motor exam: 5/5 deltoids, triceps, grip, wrist extension bilaterally; 4/5 hip flexion bilaterally, 4-/5 ankle dorsiflexion bilaterally, 4/5 knee extension bilaterally, 4/5 plantar flexion bilaterally. Sensory: decreased pinprick in the medial thighs, groin, and perineum in a saddle distribution, preserved in lateral legs, decreased light touch over the same distribution. DTRs 2+ biceps, 1+ knees, absent ankles bilaterally. Toes downgoing. Rectal tone decreased. Bulbocavernosus reflex absent. She has not voided spontaneously since this morning; bladder scan shows 520 mL post-void residual.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY AND CONSTRAINTS:
- Claustrophobia: moderate. She had an incomplete outpatient MRI six years ago (required oral lorazepam 1 mg and only partial imaging obtained). She could try again with oral lorazepam pre-scan but anxiety is likely to affect cooperation in a prolonged protocol. Anesthesia-assisted MRI is available with 45-minute notice at our institution.
- Renal function: eGFR 28. Baseline CKD 4, no dialysis. Creatinine baseline 1.9, today 2.1. Not on metformin. Hydration status adequate on arrival per bedside assessment. She is not currently on ACE-inhibitor or ARB.
- Contrast allergy: one prior mild iodinated reaction four years ago (hives only, responded to oral diphenhydramine), no prior severe reaction. No known gadolinium reaction. She has not had gadolinium in the last 6 years.
- Implants: programmable VP shunt placed 14 years ago for adult-onset hydrocephalus (etiology presumed post-meningitic). Manufacturer documented on the shunt card she carries (Medtronic Strata); valve is MR-conditional up to 3T with post-scan reprogramming requirement. Current setting confirmed in chart from last adjustment 9 months ago at 1.5 performance level. She has had MRI at 1.5T previously without incident and reprogramming was done afterward.
- Other surgical history: bilateral knee arthroplasty 2019 (both documented MR-conditional by device cards in chart). Prior cholecystectomy. No pacemaker. No retained metallic fragments. Prior orbital foreign body screening was done at the time of her last MRI and documented. No cochlear implants.
- Pregnancy: not applicable (postmenopausal, age 54, amenorrheic for several years).
- Sedation: anesthesia available with 45-minute notice. She can take oral medications. Her airway on exam is unremarkable; Mallampati class II.
- Body habitus: BMI 32 is within scanner tolerance for both modalities (our bore limits are up to BMI 40 on our 3T unit).

MEDICATIONS, ALLERGIES, SOCIAL: Medications: hydrocodone-acetaminophen 5/325 q6h PRN for pain (recently started), ibuprofen 400 mg TID for inflammation, gabapentin 300 mg TID, duloxetine 30 mg daily for chronic pain syndrome, escitalopram 10 mg daily for depression, no anticoagulation, no antiplatelet beyond aspirin 81 mg daily started by PCP for primary prevention. Allergies: iodinated contrast mild as above, NSAIDs cause GI upset but not anaphylaxis, NKDA otherwise. Social: retired office administrator, lives with spouse, two adult children, no tobacco, no alcohol, no recreational drugs. She is anxious about the exam and about the possibility of surgery.

PRIOR IMAGING AT OUR INSTITUTION: MRI lumbar spine 6 years ago without contrast (the incomplete scan) showed moderate degenerative changes without focal lesion; the scan terminated midway due to her anxiety. CT lumbar spine 3 years ago without contrast was performed after a fall and showed stable degenerative changes without fracture. No prior imaging of the thoracic spine at our institution. No prior shunt series imaging. No prior gadolinium studies documented. Outside records indicate one abdominal ultrasound 5 years ago at another facility.

CLINICAL STAKES AND DIFFERENTIAL: Spinal epidural abscess requires urgent MRI whole-spine with and without contrast as the gold-standard imaging. Non-contrast MRI can detect the abscess but does not characterize enhancement patterns or differentiate phlegmon from mature collection as well. CT myelogram is an alternative but requires LP, has iodinated contrast, and does not characterize soft-tissue involvement as well. Non-contrast CT spine is inadequate for epidural space evaluation. The differential also includes intramedullary inflammatory or neoplastic disease (less likely given the acuity and systemic picture), compressive disc herniation with radicular plus cauda equina syndrome (possible but the febrile presentation and inflammatory markers favor infectious), and paraneoplastic myelopathy (unlikely). IV antibiotics have not yet been started because blood cultures are still incubating. Infectious disease is aware.

TIMING: If surgical decompression is needed to prevent further neurologic loss, the imaging decision needs to happen in the next 60-90 minutes. Anesthesia support for claustrophobia management can be stood up in 45 minutes. Neurosurgery has indicated they will act on contrast-enhanced MRI findings; they would accept non-contrast MRI for triage but strongly prefer contrast.

OPTIONS:
1. MRI whole-spine with and without gadolinium (Group II macrocyclic, smallest diagnostic dose). eGFR < 30 is a relative concern. The benefit-risk favors imaging given surgical stakes, but the decision should be explicit and documented, and the smallest diagnostic dose should be used. Anesthesia-assisted for claustrophobia is appropriate.
2. MRI whole-spine without contrast, with anesthesia-assisted claustrophobia management. Avoids gadolinium entirely but reduces sensitivity for ring-enhancing lesion characterization. Acceptable triage study; surgery might proceed on presumed abscess without contrast-enhanced confirmation if clinical picture and non-contrast imaging align.
3. CT myelogram if MRI is truly not feasible. Requires iodinated contrast via LP; must address her prior mild reaction and current eGFR 28. Premedication is possible but the LP itself carries risk; not first-line.
4. Any choice above requires post-scan VP shunt reprogramming confirmation at a Medtronic Strata valve after 3T MRI.

DETAILED CLAUSTROPHOBIA AND MRI-COOPERATION HISTORY: Her prior incomplete MRI was an outpatient lumbar study at another facility. She entered the bore, remained approximately 8 minutes, and requested withdrawal due to anxiety. Oral lorazepam 1 mg had been given 45 minutes prior. She completed a lumbar-only scan (limited coverage) with some sequences omitted. Since that event she has avoided MRI and has not attempted another scan. Our 3T unit is a wide-bore scanner that reduces perceived confinement. Music through headphones and continuous staff voice contact via intercom are available. For full whole-spine imaging, the expected scan time is 35-45 minutes; anesthesia-supported MRI requires placement of a monitored anesthesia care provider and an IV anesthetic adjusted to deep sedation without airway compromise.

SHUNT DETAIL AND REPROGRAMMING LOGISTICS: Her Medtronic Strata valve is a magnetically adjustable programmable valve that may have its pressure setting altered by exposure to the static magnetic field of MRI. Post-scan reprogramming confirmation requires a handheld interrogator that our neurosurgery team maintains; the on-call neurosurgery resident has one available. The reprogrammer reads the current pressure setting and allows reset to the patient-specific therapeutic setting (performance level 1.5 in this case). Turnaround is typically 10-15 minutes after scan completion. Documentation of the post-scan setting confirmation is required in the chart per institutional policy.

ANESTHESIA ASSESSMENT: Airway: Mallampati II, thyromental distance adequate, neck mobility normal, no beard, no obvious airway concerns. Weight 89 kg. Fasting status: NPO since 22:00 last night. ASA class III given CKD 4. No prior anesthesia complications. Propofol or dexmedetomidine are acceptable depending on hemodynamic stability. The anesthesia resident is available with 45 minutes notice; the attending is on call.

PROGRESSION TIMELINE AND NEURO-DETERIORATION TRAJECTORY: Her back pain started approximately 6 days ago, described initially as dull and localized midline. Within 48 hours it became more severe and she began having trouble standing from a seated position. Lower-extremity weakness became noticeable on day 3-4. Saddle paresthesia and the new urinary retention emerged today. Her spouse brought her in when she could not stand without assistance this morning.

PRIMARY CARE AND RECENT HEALTHCARE CONTACTS: She has a primary care physician at our network whom she last saw six weeks ago for a routine visit. At that visit she mentioned intermittent back pain but it had been attributed to chronic mechanical low back pain. She was offered physical therapy referral which she had not yet initiated. No acute imaging was obtained at that visit.

FAMILY AND SOCIAL SUPPORT: Her spouse is at the bedside. Two adult children live in the area and have been notified. Her spouse is her healthcare proxy per the advance directive. She lives in a two-story home; the bedroom is upstairs. If she is discharged with residual weakness, home modifications or rehabilitation placement may be required. This operational consideration is flagged for the care coordinator.
